text: Ventral hernia repairs, especially incisional hernias like this one, require a tailored and individual approach.

Robotic surgery has provided the tools to close multiple large defects, suture in a large piece of mesh, and conceal the mesh from the abdominal contents using a preperitoneal pocket, something that is not possible using standard laparoscopic approaches.
The surgeon begins the operation by creating a preperitoneal pocket, preparing the surgical field for further steps in the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the paper-thin layer of the abdominal wall from the posterior rectus fascia, revealing the strength layer of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs meticulous dissection to preserve the integrity of the thin peritoneal lining and the robust posterior rectus fascia.
The surgeon positions the thin peritoneal lining to serve as a biological barrier between the implanted mesh and the intestine, ensuring separation of these structures.
During the dissection, the surgeon discovers another hernia, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces the hernia and incorporates it into the larger repair, ensuring it is positioned within the preperitoneal pocket created earlier.
This is not an uncommon occurrence with ventral incisional hernias, and this demonstrates another benefit to completely exposing the posterior rectus fascia.
The surgeon deploys the mesh into the surgical field after creating an adequate pocket, ensuring it is positioned beneath the thin peritoneal lining and robust posterior rectus fascia.
The surgeon assesses a large hernia defect, preparing to cover it with a fifteen-centimeter piece of mesh.
The surgeon uses a self-retaining suture to close the large abdominal wall hernia, securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon prepares to close a larger hernia defect, potentially using multiple sutures to ensure complete closure of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon places three sutures to close the defect of the large abdominal wall hernia, ensuring adequate closure.
The surgeon unrolls the mesh and positions it centrally over the repaired abdominal wall, ensuring it covers the defect adequately.
The surgeon sutures the mesh directly to the posterior rectus fascia, securing it in place to prevent migration.
The surgeon closes the preperitoneal pocket using an absorbable self-retaining suture, securing the tissue layers within the abdominal wall.
The mesh is fully concealed beneath the abdominal wall, providing a protective barrier over the intestines.
Adhesions and erosion of the mesh into the intestine is virtually impossible with this technique.
The surgeon removes the needles from the abdomen and closes the twelve millimeter port site, completing the closure of the surgical access point.
